changePlan.question: Can I change my plan at any time?
changePlan.answer: Yes, you can upgrade or downgrade your plan at any time. When upgrading, you get access to new features immediately. When downgrading, the new plan takes effect from the next billing period.

athleteLimit.question: What happens if I have more athletes than my plan allows?
athleteLimit.answer: You keep access to all existing athletes, but you cannot add new ones until you either upgrade or remove some athletes.

payment.question: How does payment work?
payment.answer: We use Stripe for secure payments. You can pay by card and will be billed automatically every month or year depending on your chosen billing period.

pricing.title: Pricing
pricing.subtitle: Choose the plan that fits your needs

trial.question: Is there a free trial?
trial.answer: Yes! The Starter plan is completely free and you can use it as long as you want with up to 2 athletes.

changePlan.question: Can I change my plan at any time?
changePlan.answer: Yes, you can upgrade or downgrade your plan at any time. When upgrading, you get access to new features immediately.

payment.question: What payment methods are accepted?
payment.answer: We accept all major credit cards via Stripe. Invoicing is also available for Business and Enterprise plans.

cancelAnytime.question: Can I cancel at any time?
cancelAnytime.answer: Yes, you can cancel your subscription at any time. You keep access to the service until the end of your billing period.

paceZones.title: Practical pace zones
paceZones.description: Based on your threshold values, these are the recommended paces for different training types:
paceZones.note: These paces are calculated from your individual threshold values. Heart-rate zones provide more precise guidance during training, especially in variable conditions.

raceFuelingEstimate.title: Race fueling
raceFuelingEstimate.description: An estimate of carbohydrate needs at race-like intensity based on metabolic test data.

validation.needTarget: Enter expected time, or distance together with speed/pace.

buildUp.title: Gut training until race day
buildUp.description: Build from {start} to {target} g/h with one race-like session per week.

interpretation.title: Interpretation
interpretation.description: The body can burn more carbohydrates than the gut can absorb. The recommendation is therefore a practical intake target, while estimated carb demand shows the load on glycogen stores.

testScope.title: About this test
testScope.description: This test was completed without VO₂ measurement. Lactate and heart-rate measurements are sufficient for calculating training zones and threshold values. VO₂ measurement is only required for VO₂max assessment and running economy analysis.

postTest.title: Post-measurements (post-max lactate)
postTest.description: Peak lactate after maximum load gives an indication of anaerobic capacity

dmax.title: About the D-max method
dmax.description: D-max is a mathematical method that identifies the lactate threshold by finding the point with maximum distance from the baseline to the measured lactate curve. This is especially important for well-trained athletes with "flat" lactate curves, where the traditional 4 mmol/L method can overestimate the threshold and lead to overtraining. R² shows how well the polynomial curve fits your data ({fit} fit).

substrate.title: Substrate utilization
substrate.description: The chart shows the share of fat and carbohydrate combustion at each intensity. The crossover point is where carbohydrate combustion overtakes fat combustion — below this intensity, fat oxidation dominates, which is important for base training and endurance.

runningEconomy.title: Running economy
runningEconomy.description1: Running economy measures how efficiently your body uses oxygen while running. The value is given in ml O₂ per kg body weight per kilometer (ml/kg/km) and shows how much oxygen is required to run one kilometer at a given speed.
runningEconomy.description2: Lower value = better economy. A runner with good economy uses less energy at the same speed compared with a runner with poorer economy.
runningEconomy.improvementNote: Running economy can be improved through plyometric training, strength training for legs and core, and technical running drills focused on cadence optimization.

descriptions.swimming: Good sleep, good food, and calm mobility today improve the quality of your next swim.
descriptions.cycling: Load up with sleep, energy, and leg recovery so your next ride has the right pressure.
descriptions.triathlon: Recover systematically today so you can meet the next discipline with better quality.
descriptions.hyrox: Give your body a day to restore strength, engine, and grip before the next HYROX workout.
descriptions.functional: Recovery today creates better quality in your next session with strength, heart rate, and technique.
descriptions.strength: Good recovery today improves power development and quality in your next strength session.
descriptions.skiing: Quality sleep and good energy today create better pressure and technique in your next ski session.
descriptions.racket: Light recovery today improves timing, footwork, and quality in your next racket session.
descriptions.team: Recovery today improves explosiveness and quality in your next team training.

low.swimming: Prioritize sleep, fluids, and light mobility for shoulders and thoracic spine so you are fresh for your next swim.
low.cycling: Focus on leg and hip recovery plus energy intake so you are ready for the next quality ride.
low.triathlon: Let your body absorb today's load. Simple mobility and good energy help you come back stronger across all three disciplines.
low.functional: Recovery today improves quality in the next combination of strength and engine. Stick to light mobility and good food.
low.strength: Give your nervous system, muscles, and joints time to recover today so the next strength session can be done with quality.
low.skiing: Prioritize calm recovery, fuel, and mobility for hips, ankles, and upper body before the next ski session.
low.racket: Recover lower legs, shoulders, and trunk today so your next session with direction changes and shots has better quality.
low.team: Let tendons, muscles, and nervous system recover today so you can be quick and explosive in the next team session.
low.running: Give tendons, calves, and feet calm loading today so your next run has better quality.
low.default: Prioritize sleep, fluids, and light mobility today so your body has time to absorb the training.

normal.swimming: Use the rest day to reset shoulders, back, and energy so your next swim has better quality.
normal.cycling: The rest day helps your legs respond better to the next threshold, VO2, or endurance ride.
normal.triathlon: Recovery today strengthens your whole system across swimming, cycling, and running, making the next key session more valuable.
normal.hyrox: The rest day lets strength, engine, and durability build before the next HYROX workout.
normal.functional: Recovery today improves quality in your next session with strength, heart rate, and technique.
normal.strength: Muscles get stronger when you recover. A calm day today improves quality in the next lift.
normal.skiing: The rest day creates room for better technique and power development in the next ski or poling session.
normal.racket: Recovery today helps you be quicker in footwork and sharper in timing next session.
normal.team: The rest day improves explosiveness, decision-making, and durability for the next team training.
normal.running: Your muscles, tendons, and energy systems build capacity while you rest before the next run.
normal.default: Your muscles adapt and get stronger while you rest.

open.low: Your status points to low load today. Choose mobility, light circulation, or low-stress technique.

high.cycling: Good day for a short quality ride, cadence work, or a controlled threshold block.
high.swimming: Good day for technique sets, pace feel, or a short quality swim.
high.triathlon: Choose a clear discipline today, such as a short quality session or transition work with control.
high.functional: Good moment for technique under heart rate, station work, or a short strength-and-engine session.
high.strength: Good day for a focused strength session with quality in the main lifts or accessory work.
high.skiing: Good day for poling strength, technique intervals, or a short quality session on rollerskis/skierg.
high.racket: Good day for footwork, direction changes, and short intense sequences with strong quality.
high.team: Good day for accelerations, match-situation technique, or a short strength-and-power session.
high.running: Good day for strides, hill technique, or a short quality run if you want to keep rhythm.
high.default: Your status looks good for a short quality session, technique block, or focused strength.

normal.cycling: An easy endurance ride, trainer technique, or hip and ankle mobility fits well today.
normal.swimming: Focus on technique, body position, and calm aerobic volume if you want to train today.
normal.triathlon: A calm discipline in zone 1-2 or technique work gives the best effect without disrupting the whole plan.
normal.functional: Keep it simple today: light engine, mobility, and technically clean reps.
normal.strength: Technique sets, trunk stability, and light accessory strength fit better today than high load.
normal.skiing: Calm aerobic training and technique focus give the most value today.
normal.racket: Prioritize movement quality, lighter shot volume, and injury-prevention work today.
normal.team: Light footwork, mobility, and injury-prevention work fit better than extra high-intensity volume today.
normal.running: An easy jog, walk, or running drills gives more value than chasing extra load today.
normal.default: Low-intensity training, technique work, or mobility fits best today.
